walkthrough.title: Unfold the Board
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Las Vegas. Let's get Lords of Vegas set up. First, unfold the game board and lay it flat in the center of the table. You will see a map of The Strip with a grid of rectangular lots organized into blocks labeled A through F. Each lot has a letter-number code, a build cost printed on it, and a color. The scoring track runs along the edge of the board. Make sure everyone can see and reach the board comfortably.

walkthrough.title: Choose Player Colors and Distribute Components
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color. There are four options: the exact colors depend on your edition, but each player color has its own set of twelve dice and ten markers. Take all twelve dice and all ten markers of your chosen color and keep them in front of you. The dice will represent your workers inside casinos, and the markers show which lots you own on the board. Also give each player an action reference card that summarizes what you can do on your turn.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Casino Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the casino tiles. There are five colors of casino tiles: aqua, brown, gold, purple, and silver. Each color has nine tiles. Sort them into five separate stacks by color and place them near the board where everyone can reach them. These tiles will be placed on lots when players build casinos.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Money Supply
walkthrough.text: Organize the money. The bills come in three denominations: one million, five million, and ten million dollars. Stack them neatly near the board to form the bank. Choose one player to act as the banker. They will handle payouts and collect payments throughout the game. In Lords of Vegas, money is tight and every dollar matters, so keep the bank organized.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Property Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the property card deck. There are 49 regular property cards plus one special End of Game card. Set the End of Game card aside for now. Shuffle the 49 property cards thoroughly. These cards each show a lot on the board with its block letter, number, build cost, and color. During the game, drawing these cards determines which lots come into play and which casino colors score.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Properties
walkthrough.text: Deal two property cards face up to each player. Look at the lots shown on your two cards. Place one of your colored markers on each of those two lots on the board. You now own those lots. Next, add up the dollar values printed on your two cards. Take that total amount in cash from the bank. For example, if your cards show lots worth three million and five million, you take eight million dollars. After everyone has placed markers and taken money, discard these starting cards face up into a discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Insert the End of Game Card
walkthrough.text: Now take the remaining property deck and count out roughly the bottom quarter of the cards. Insert the End of Game card somewhere randomly into that bottom quarter. You do not need to be precise. The point is that the End of Game card will appear somewhere in the last portion of the deck, so nobody knows exactly when the game will end. Square up the deck and place it face down near the board.

walkthrough.title: Note the Scoring Track
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to look at the scoring track around the edge of the board. Notice the small lines or breaks at certain point values. These breaks are important because when a casino scores, you must have enough points to clear the next break to advance past it. If you do not, excess points are lost. Each player places one of their markers on zero on the scoring track. The player who most recently visited Las Vegas goes first, or just pick someone randomly. You are ready to play.

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do two steps in order. Step one is the Draw phase, which is mandatory. Step two is the Play phase, where you can take actions. The Draw phase generates income for everyone at the table, not just you. Then in the Play phase, you spend money to build your casino empire. Turns go clockwise around the table until the End of Game card is drawn.

walkthrough.title: Step 1: Draw a Card
walkthrough.text: Draw the top card of the property deck and flip it face up. This card shows a specific lot on the board. If that lot is empty and unowned, you place one of your markers on it. You now own that lot for free. If someone else already has a marker there, you replace their marker with yours and place one of your dice showing a one on that lot. You just took over. If the lot already has a casino tile on it, place your die showing a one on the tile. You are now part of that casino.

walkthrough.title: Lots Pay Cash
walkthrough.text: After the card is drawn and the lot is claimed, every player collects one million dollars from the bank for each lot they own that has a marker on it. This is not just the active player. Everyone with markers on the board gets paid. Lots with casino tiles and dice do not count for this payment. Only bare markers on empty lots pay out. This is your passive income, and it happens every single turn, so the more lots you hold, the more cash flows in.

walkthrough.title: Casinos Score and Pay
walkthrough.text: Now check the color of the card you just drew. Every casino on the board that matches that color scores points and pays money. The boss of each matching casino scores one victory point per tile in their casino. Then every player with dice in that casino earns one million dollars per pip showing on their dice. For example, if you have a die showing four in a gold casino and a gold card is drawn, you collect four million dollars. If you are the boss of that casino and it has three tiles, you also score three points on the track. If the card says Pay The Strip, every casino adjacent to The Strip scores and pays instead, regardless of color.

walkthrough.title: Step 2: Play Phase — Build
walkthrough.text: Now you enter the Play phase, where you can perform actions in any order and as many times as you like, provided you can pay. The most basic action is Build. To build, choose one of your lots that has only a marker on it, with no tile. Pay the build cost printed on that lot to the bank. Then place a casino tile of any color you choose on the lot, and place one of your dice on top showing any value you want. You are now the boss of a brand new one-tile casino. The color you choose matters a lot because it determines when your casino scores.

walkthrough.title: Sprawl
walkthrough.text: Sprawl lets the boss of a casino expand it into an adjacent empty, unowned lot. The lot must share a border with the casino, not just a corner. You pay double the build cost printed on that adjacent lot. Then place a tile matching your casino's color on that lot and put one of your dice on it showing any value you want. The new tile becomes part of your existing casino. Only the boss of a casino can sprawl it. This is how casinos grow from one tile into massive point-scoring empires.

walkthrough.title: Remodel
walkthrough.text: Remodel is one of the most powerful and aggressive moves in the game. Only the boss of a casino can do it. You pay five million dollars per tile in the casino. Then you swap all the tiles to a new color of your choice. Every tile changes at once. This does not move any dice. Here is why it is devastating: if your casino is now adjacent to an opponent's casino of the same new color, those casinos merge into one. The boss is determined by whoever has the highest single die. You can remodel to absorb an opponent's casino and potentially become boss of the whole thing.

walkthrough.title: Reorganize
walkthrough.text: Reorganize lets you fight for control of a casino where you have at least one die. You pay one million dollars per pip on all dice in the entire casino, not just yours. Then every die in that casino, belonging to all players, is rerolled. The new boss is whoever now has the highest single die. This is a gamble. You might seize control, or you might waste your money and end up worse off. If there is a tie for highest die after the reroll, only the tied dice are rerolled repeatedly until the tie breaks.

walkthrough.title: Gamble
walkthrough.text: You can gamble once per turn at another player's casino where you are not the boss. You bet up to five million dollars per tile in that casino. Then you roll two six-sided dice and add them together. If the total is two or twelve, you win double your bet from the bank. If the total is three, four, nine, ten, or eleven, you win your bet amount from the bank. If the total is five, six, seven, or eight, you lose your bet to the bank. The boss of the casino you gambled at can lay off half of your winnings, meaning they can take half from the bank before you collect. Gambling is a side hustle, not a core strategy, but it can provide crucial cash at the right moment.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Track and Breaks
walkthrough.text: The scoring track has breaks at certain point thresholds. When you score points from a casino, you advance along the track. But if you hit a break and do not have enough points to cross it completely, any leftover points are lost. For example, if you are at seven points and a break is at ten, you need at least three points to advance past it. If you only score two, you stay at seven and those two points vanish. When scoring multiple casinos of the same color at once, score the smallest casino first. This matters because smaller scores might get you past a break, allowing a larger score to count fully afterward.

walkthrough.title: Boss Mechanics and Tie-Breaking
walkthrough.text: The boss of a casino is the player with the single highest die value in that casino. Not the most dice, not the highest total. Just the single biggest die. If there is a tie, the tied players immediately reroll only their tied highest dice until someone wins. The boss is the only player who scores victory points when the casino's color comes up. The boss is also the only one who can sprawl or remodel that casino. Non-boss players with dice in a casino still earn money based on their dice pips, but they score zero points.

walkthrough.title: Trading and Game End
walkthrough.text: At any time during your turn, you can trade with other players. You can trade money, lots with markers, dice on tiles, and even future actions. The only things you cannot trade are victory points and casino tiles themselves. Be creative. Now for the end of the game: when someone draws the End of Game card instead of a property card, the game is almost over. There is one final Strip payment, where every casino adjacent to The Strip scores and pays one last time. Then the player with the most victory points wins. If tied, the player with the most money wins. Also, if any player ever reaches ninety points or more, they win immediately, no matter whose turn it is.

Step 2: Play phase — perform actions in any order, any number of times

walkthrough.title: Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through two practice turns with three players to see how Lords of Vegas flows. Player 1 is Alex, playing red. Player 2 is Blake, playing blue. Player 3 is Casey, playing yellow. During setup, Alex received lots B3 and D5, Blake received lots A2 and C4, and Casey received lots C2 and E1. Everyone placed their markers on those lots and collected starting money based on their card values. The deck is shuffled with the End of Game card buried in the bottom quarter. Alex goes first.

walkthrough.title: Alex Draws a Card
walkthrough.text: Alex draws the top card of the property deck. It shows lot C1, which is a gold lot. Nobody owns C1 yet, so Alex places a red marker on that lot. Alex now owns three lots: B3, D5, and the newly claimed C1.

walkthrough.title: Everyone Gets Paid for Lots
walkthrough.text: Now all players collect one million dollars per lot they own with a marker on it. Alex has three markers, so Alex collects three million. Blake has two markers on A2 and C4, so Blake collects two million. Casey has two markers on C2 and E1, so Casey collects two million. This happens every single turn, no matter whose turn it is. Do not forget this step.

walkthrough.title: Gold Casinos Score
walkthrough.text: The card Alex drew was gold. All gold casinos on the board would now score points and pay money. But nobody has built any casinos yet, so nothing happens this time. As the game progresses, these color payments become the main source of both points and income. Keep an eye on which colors have been drawn recently because the same color can come up multiple times.

walkthrough.title: Alex Builds a Casino
walkthrough.text: Now Alex enters the Play phase. Alex decides to build on lot C1, which has a build cost of four million. Alex pays four million to the bank, takes a gold casino tile, and places it on lot C1. Then Alex places one of their red dice on top showing a six. Alex chose gold because the card that just came up was gold, and Alex wants to score when gold comes up again. Alex chose to show a six on the die to make sure they are the boss of this casino. Alex now has a one-tile gold casino and is its boss.

walkthrough.title: Alex's Turn Ends
walkthrough.text: Alex has no more money to spend or decides to save the rest. Alex's turn is over. Notice that Alex still owns lots B3 and D5 with markers on them. Those lots will keep paying one million each every turn until Alex builds on them or someone else claims them. Play passes to Blake.

walkthrough.title: Blake Draws a Card
walkthrough.text: Blake draws a card showing lot B1, which is a purple lot. Nobody owns it, so Blake places a blue marker on B1. Now for lot payments: Alex has two markers (B3 and D5) since C1 now has a tile, so Alex gets two million. Blake has three markers (A2, C4, and the new B1), so Blake gets three million. Casey still has two markers, so Casey gets two million.

walkthrough.title: Purple Casinos Score
walkthrough.text: The drawn card is purple. Are there any purple casinos? No, nobody has built a purple casino yet. Nothing scores. Blake makes a mental note that purple has now come up, so building a purple casino could pay off soon if another purple card appears.

walkthrough.title: Blake Builds on C4
walkthrough.text: Blake decides to build on lot C4. Blake pays the build cost, places a brown casino tile, and puts a blue die on top showing a five. Why brown? Blake checked the discard pile and noticed no brown cards have come up yet, meaning all nine brown cards are still in the deck. That is a lot of potential scoring opportunities. Blake is thinking ahead.

walkthrough.title: Blake Forgets the Lot Payment
walkthrough.text: Wait, let's rewind. Actually, before Blake built, the group almost skipped the lot payment step. Blake only collected for two lots instead of three. This is the most commonly forgotten rule in Lords of Vegas. Every turn, after the card is drawn and the lot is claimed, ALL players collect one million per marker. It happens on every player's turn, not just your own. Set a habit of going around the table each turn: how many markers does everyone have? Pay them all. Do not skip this step.

walkthrough.title: Casey Draws a Card
walkthrough.text: Casey's turn. Casey draws a card showing lot C3, which is a gold lot. Nobody owns C3, so Casey places a yellow marker there. Lot payments: Alex gets two million (B3, D5), Blake gets two million (A2, B1 since C4 has a tile now), and Casey gets three million (C2, E1, and the new C3).

walkthrough.title: Alex's Gold Casino Scores
walkthrough.text: The drawn card is gold. Alex has a gold casino on C1. Alex is the boss, and the casino has one tile, so Alex scores one victory point on the scoring track. Alex also has one die showing six, so Alex earns six million dollars from the bank. That single tile just paid out nicely. Casey now sees why Alex chose gold. Building a casino in a color that comes up frequently means steady points and income.

walkthrough.title: Casey Builds on C2
walkthrough.text: Casey enters the Play phase and builds on lot C2. Casey pays the build cost and places a gold tile with a yellow die showing four. Casey is building gold because gold has already come up twice and C2 is right next to Alex's gold casino on C1. Casey has plans. Two separate one-tile gold casinos now sit adjacent to each other, one belonging to Alex and one to Casey.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Begins — Alex Draws Again
walkthrough.text: Back to Alex. Alex draws a card showing lot A4, a silver lot. Alex places a marker. Lot payments go around. No silver casinos exist, so no scoring. Alex decides to sprawl their gold casino on C1.

walkthrough.title: Alex Sprawls
walkthrough.text: Alex wants to sprawl the gold casino on C1 into an adjacent empty lot. Alex checks the lots bordering C1. Lot C3 is adjacent, but Casey owns it with a marker. That lot is owned, so Alex cannot sprawl there. Alex finds another adjacent lot that is empty and unowned. Alex pays double the build cost of that lot, places a gold tile on it, and puts a red die showing five. Alex's casino is now two tiles of gold with two red dice. As boss, Alex will score two points next time gold hits.

walkthrough.title: Blake Draws — and Tries to Sprawl
walkthrough.text: Blake draws a card, claims the lot, payments go around, and the color scores if applicable. Blake now wants to sprawl their brown casino on C4. But wait. Blake checks the rules. Only the boss of a casino can sprawl, and Blake is the boss. Good. But the adjacent lots are either owned by other players or are not empty. Blake realizes there is one empty unowned lot adjacent. Blake pays double the build cost and expands the brown casino to two tiles. Nice move.

walkthrough.title: Blake's Sprawl Mistake
walkthrough.text: Hold on. Blake tried to sprawl into a lot that shares only a corner with the casino, not a full edge. Sprawl requires the new lot to share a border, meaning a full side, not just a diagonal touch. Blake picks a different adjacent lot that properly shares a side with the casino. This is an easy mistake to make, especially on a crowded board. Always double-check that the lots share an actual edge.

walkthrough.title: Casey Remodels for a Hostile Takeover
walkthrough.text: Casey's turn. Casey draws, claims, payments happen. Now Casey does something aggressive. Casey's gold casino on C2 is adjacent to Alex's gold casino. Since they are the same color and adjacent, they are actually already one merged casino. Wait, that is not right either. They were built separately. In Lords of Vegas, a casino is a contiguous group of same-color tiles. If Casey's gold tile on C2 is touching Alex's gold tile on C1, they already merged when Casey built it. Let's say they are not adjacent for this example. So Casey remodels.

walkthrough.title: Casey Executes the Remodel
walkthrough.text: Casey is the boss of a one-tile gold casino on C2 (die showing four). Casey wants to remodel it to brown, the same color as Blake's casino on C4. If C2 and C4 are adjacent, changing Casey's tile to brown would merge the two casinos. Casey pays five million dollars (five per tile, one tile). Casey swaps the gold tile for a brown tile. Now Casey's brown tile on C2 is adjacent to Blake's brown tiles. They merge into one casino. The boss is determined by the highest single die. Blake has a five, Casey has a four. Blake is still boss. But Casey is now inside Blake's casino and earning money from it. And Casey only needs one good reorganize to potentially steal it.

walkthrough.title: Alex Reorganizes to Fight Back
walkthrough.text: On Alex's next turn, after the draw phase, Alex decides to reorganize. Alex has a die in a casino where another player is boss. Alex pays one million per pip on all dice in that casino. Let's say there are three dice totaling twelve pips, so Alex pays twelve million. That is expensive. All three dice are rerolled. Alex rolls a six. The other players roll a three and a two. Alex is the new boss. Reorganize is a gamble, literally. You pay a fixed cost and the outcome is random. But seizing control of a large casino is worth it if the payout on the next scoring round is big.

walkthrough.title: The Scoring Track in Action
walkthrough.text: Let's look at the scoring track. Alex has scored a few points from gold casino payouts. Alex is at four points. The next break on the track is at six. If Alex scores two or more points, Alex will cross that break and advance. If Alex only scores one point, Alex advances to five but does not cross the break, so that is fine. The break means you need enough points in a single scoring event to clear it. Keep this in mind when deciding whether to build small casinos or invest in growing large ones.

walkthrough.title: A Quick Trade
walkthrough.text: Between actions, Blake offers Casey a deal. Blake will pay Casey three million dollars if Casey agrees not to reorganize Blake's casino this turn. Casey accepts. Trades in Lords of Vegas can be anything: money for lots, lots for promises, dice swaps for future considerations. You cannot trade victory points or casino tiles, but nearly everything else is on the table. Use deals to gain advantages that pure money cannot buy.

walkthrough.title: Practice Round Wrap-Up
walkthrough.text: After two turns each, the board is already getting interesting. Alex has a multi-tile gold casino. Blake has a growing brown casino with Casey embedded in it. Casey is scheming. Lots are being claimed every turn, passive income is flowing, and the casino colors are starting to matter. Every card draw changes the landscape. That is the feel of Lords of Vegas: plans collide with luck, and every turn brings a new opportunity or threat.

walkthrough.title: You Are Ready to Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you jump into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for quick definitions of every action, component, and casino mechanic.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun building your Vegas empire!

summary.title: You Are Ready to Play
bullets: Check General Tips for strategy basics
Use the Appendix for quick reference
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Build Early, Build Often
walkthrough.text: The sooner you get casino tiles on the board, the sooner you start earning points and money when that color is drawn. An unbuilt lot only pays one million per turn. A built casino can pay many millions and score victory points. In the early game, cash is tight, but investing in a casino pays dividends every time its color appears. Do not sit on lots waiting for the perfect moment. Build and start collecting.

walkthrough.title: Sprawl Defensively
walkthrough.text: Sprawling is not just about making your casino bigger. It also blocks opponents from building next to you. If you leave empty lots adjacent to your casino, someone can build there in your color and force a merge, potentially stealing boss from you. By sprawling into those lots yourself, you control the borders. A two-tile casino with no vulnerable adjacencies is safer than a three-tile casino surrounded by enemy lots.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Discard Pile
walkthrough.text: This is one of the biggest edges in the game. Every time a property card is drawn, it goes face up into the discard pile. There are nine cards of each casino color plus some Strip cards. By tracking which colors have already appeared, you know which colors are more likely to come up next. If seven gold cards have been drawn and only two remain, building a new gold casino is a poor investment. If zero brown cards have appeared, brown is going to pay out repeatedly.

walkthrough.title: Remodel for Hostile Takeovers
walkthrough.text: Remodeling is the most aggressive tool in your arsenal. If your casino is adjacent to an opponent's casino, you can remodel your tiles to match their color, forcing a merge. If your highest die beats theirs, you just became boss of the combined casino. Even if you do not win boss immediately, you can follow up with a reorganize to fight for it. The threat of remodeling also has value. Opponents will think twice about building next to you.

walkthrough.title: Keep Cash Reserves
walkthrough.text: Going broke in Lords of Vegas is devastating. You cannot build, sprawl, remodel, or reorganize without money. And if an opponent remodels into your casino and you cannot afford to reorganize, you just watch them take your points. Try to keep a reserve of at least five to ten million at all times. Passive income from lot markers helps, but do not spend every dollar the moment you get it. The best opportunities often come unexpectedly.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Breaks Matter
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to where you are on the scoring track and where the next break is. If you are one point below a break, you need a casino big enough to push you over in a single scoring event. Sometimes it is better to build up one large casino rather than spreading across several small ones. A four-tile casino scoring four points will jump you past breaks that two separate two-tile casinos never could, since they score independently.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Ignore Trading
walkthrough.text: Trading is an underused tool, especially among newer players. Offering another player a few million dollars to leave your casino alone, or swapping a lot for a strategic advantage, can be more efficient than spending money on actions. If someone has a lot adjacent to your casino that you want to sprawl into, consider buying it from them rather than waiting and hoping. Everything is negotiable except points and tiles.

summary.title: Build Early, Build Often
bullets: Unbuilt lots pay only $1M — casinos pay much more and score VP
Invest in casinos early to start earning dividends

summary.title: Sprawl Defensively
bullets: Sprawl to block opponents from merging into your casino
Control the borders around your casinos

summary.title: Watch the Discard Pile
bullets: 9 cards per color — track what has been drawn
Build in colors with many remaining cards in the deck

summary.title: Remodel for Hostile Takeovers
bullets: Match an adjacent opponent's color to force a merge
Follow up with reorganize if you are not boss after the merge

summary.title: Keep Cash Reserves
bullets: Keep $5-10M in reserve for defensive reorganizes and opportunities
Going broke means you cannot respond to threats

summary.title: Scoring Breaks Matter
bullets: Points below a break are lost if the scoring event is too small
One large casino crosses breaks better than several small ones

summary.title: Do Not Ignore Trading
bullets: Buy lots, sell truces, negotiate alliances
Trading can be more efficient than paying for actions

walkthrough.title: Color Counting Is Everything
walkthrough.text: There are exactly nine property cards per casino color in the deck, plus a handful of Pay The Strip cards. Serious players track the discard pile closely. If six gold cards have been drawn and three remain in approximately twenty remaining cards, you can estimate the probability of gold scoring again. This informs every decision: which color to build, when to remodel, and whether a casino is worth investing in. The discard pile is public information. Use it.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Remodels
walkthrough.text: A remodel is most powerful when you can combine it with knowledge of remaining cards. If you remodel your casino to brown and there are seven brown cards still in the deck, you will likely score multiple times before the game ends. If you remodel to a color with only one card remaining, you might never score. The ideal remodel also forces a merge where your die is higher than the opponent's, immediately seizing boss of a larger casino. Time it for maximum damage.

walkthrough.title: Reorganize Math
walkthrough.text: Before paying for a reorganize, do the math. The cost is one million per pip on all dice. If there are four dice totaling sixteen pips, you are spending sixteen million with no guarantee of winning. Your probability of becoming boss depends on how many dice you have versus your opponents. With one die against one opponent's die, it is close to fifty-fifty. With one die against three opponent dice, you need to roll higher than all three. Factor in the expected points and money you would earn as boss, and decide if the gamble is worth the price.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Sprawling as Bait
walkthrough.text: Sometimes sprawling into a lot adjacent to an opponent's casino is a deliberate provocation. By extending your casino toward theirs, you create a merge opportunity. If your dice are strong, you want the merge because you will likely be boss of the combined mega-casino. If your opponent does not remodel to prevent it, you can remodel yourself to force the merge on your terms. This is an advanced form of board control where your sprawl is an offensive weapon, not just expansion.

walkthrough.title: Gamble Math and When to Use It
walkthrough.text: Gambling has a roughly 44.4 percent chance of winning (including the 5.6 percent chance of winning double on two or twelve). The expected value is slightly negative, which is thematic for a Vegas game. However, the boss can lay off half your winnings, reducing your effective gain. Gambling is best used when you are cash-poor but need money for a critical action, or when the casino is large enough that the five-million-per-tile maximum bet makes the potential payout worthwhile. Never gamble as your primary income strategy.

walkthrough.title: Trading Leverage
walkthrough.text: Your best trades happen when you have something an opponent desperately needs. A lot adjacent to their casino is worth far more than its face value because they need it for sprawling or defense. A promise not to reorganize is worth money if they just became boss of a large casino. The key is recognizing when you have leverage and extracting maximum value. Never give away a lot for its printed cost when it is strategically critical to another player. Make them pay a premium.

walkthrough.title: Strip Adjacency Value
walkthrough.text: Lots adjacent to The Strip are premium real estate. Pay The Strip cards cause every casino touching The Strip to score, regardless of color. This means a Strip-adjacent casino scores when its own color comes up AND when any Pay The Strip card comes up. That is effectively extra cards in the deck that trigger your scoring. When choosing where to build, prioritize Strip-adjacent lots. A Strip-adjacent casino has a higher expected scoring frequency than one further from The Strip.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Positioning
walkthrough.text: As the deck thins and the End of Game card approaches, shift your strategy. Stop investing in long-term growth and focus on maximizing your score right now. If you are near a scoring break, make sure your largest casino can push you past it. Consider reorganizing casinos where you are not boss, even at poor odds, because the points from becoming boss might win you the game. The final Strip payment after the End of Game card is drawn can swing the result, so having casinos adjacent to The Strip is extra valuable in the endgame.

walkthrough.title: Die Value Management
walkthrough.text: When you build or sprawl, you choose what value to show on your die. Always show the highest value possible, typically six, unless you have a strategic reason not to. A high die makes you boss and makes it harder for opponents to steal control via reorganize. However, higher dice also mean higher reorganize costs for everyone, including you. In rare cases, showing a lower die to reduce the total pips in a casino can make reorganization cheaper if you plan to reorganize yourself later.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what your opponents are doing. If someone is hoarding cash, they are probably planning a remodel or reorganize. If someone builds a casino right next to yours, they are positioning for a merge. If someone keeps building in one color, check the discard pile to see if that color is actually going to pay off. Anticipating threats lets you sprawl defensively, remodel pre-emptively, or negotiate from a position of strength. The best Vegas moguls read the table, not just the cards.

summary.title: Color Counting Is Everything
bullets: 9 cards per color — track the discard pile religiously
Build in colors with the most remaining cards

summary.title: Timing Your Remodels
bullets: Remodel to a color with many cards left in the deck
Combine with a forced merge where your die is highest

summary.title: Reorganize Math
bullets: Cost = $1M per pip on ALL dice in the casino
Weigh cost against expected VP and money as boss

summary.title: Strategic Sprawling as Bait
bullets: Sprawl toward opponents to set up a merge on your terms
Works best when your dice are strong

summary.title: Gamble Math and When to Use It
bullets: ~44.4% win rate, slightly negative expected value
Best as emergency cash, not a primary strategy
Boss can lay off half your winnings

summary.title: Trading Leverage
bullets: Lots adjacent to opponent casinos are worth a premium
Sell non-aggression pacts when opponents are vulnerable

summary.title: Strip Adjacency Value
bullets: Strip-adjacent casinos score on their color AND on Pay The Strip cards
Higher expected scoring frequency — prioritize these lots

summary.title: Endgame Positioning
bullets: As deck thins, shift from growth to immediate scoring
Final Strip payment can swing the game — have Strip-adjacent casinos

summary.title: Die Value Management
bullets: Show 6 by default — boss protection and harder to steal
Lower values reduce reorganize costs if you plan to reorganize yourself

summary.title: Reading Your Opponents
bullets: Cash hoarding signals a remodel or reorganize incoming
Building near you signals a merge attempt — sprawl defensively

entries.definition: Action. Place a casino tile of any color and one of your dice (at any value) on a lot you own that has only a marker. Pay the build cost printed on the lot. You become boss of a new one-tile casino.

Action. Boss only. Expand your casino into an adjacent empty, unowned lot that shares a full edge (not just a corner). Pay double the build cost of the new lot. Place a matching-color tile and your die at any value.
Action. Boss only. Change all tiles in your casino to a new color. Pay $5M per tile. Does not move dice. Can cause mergers with adjacent same-color casinos.

Action. You must have at least one die in the casino. Pay $1M per pip on ALL dice in the casino (all players' dice). All dice in the casino are rerolled. Highest single die becomes boss. Ties rerolled until resolved.

Action. Once per turn. Bet up to $5M per tile at another boss's casino where you are not boss. Roll 2d6: totals 3, 4, 9, 10, 11 win your bet; 2 or 12 win double; 5, 6, 7, 8 lose. Boss can lay off half your winnings to the bank.
Action. Available at any time during your Play phase. Trade money, lots (markers), dice on tiles, and future actions with other players. Cannot trade victory points or casino tiles.

Square tiles in five colors placed on lots when building or sprawling. A contiguous group of same-color tiles forms one casino. There are 9 tiles per color (45 total): aqua, brown, gold, purple, and silver.

Each player has 12 dice of their color. Dice are placed on casino tiles when building or sprawling, and show a pip value. The die value determines boss status and money earned during scoring. Dice are rerolled during reorganization.

Each player has 10 markers of their color. Markers are placed on lots to indicate ownership before a casino is built. Lots with markers pay $1M per turn. Markers are removed when a tile is built on the lot.

49 cards in the deck, each showing a specific lot (block letter and number), build cost, and color. When drawn, the active player claims the lot. The card's color determines which casinos score that turn. 9 cards per casino color plus Pay The Strip cards.
Special card inserted into the bottom quarter of the property deck during setup. When drawn, the game enters its final phase: one last Strip payment occurs, then the player with the most VP wins.

Bills in $1M, $5M, and $10M denominations. Used to pay for all actions (build, sprawl, remodel, reorganize, gamble). Earned from lot payments, casino scoring (per pip), and gambling wins. Tiebreaker for final scoring.

The board shows The Strip with lots organized in blocks A through F. Each lot has a letter-number code, a build cost, and a color. The scoring track runs along the edge. In 2-player games, block F is disabled.
A player aid card summarizing all available actions and their costs. One per player. Handy for quick reference during the Play phase.

Track around the board edge where players mark their victory points. Contains breaks at certain thresholds. Points from a single scoring event must be enough to cross a break; excess points below a break are lost.

Marked thresholds on the scoring track. When scoring, if you do not have enough points to advance past the next break, the excess points are lost. Score smallest casinos first to potentially clear breaks before larger casinos score.
When a casino's color is drawn (or Pay The Strip triggers), the boss scores 1 VP per tile in the casino. Non-boss players score zero VP but still earn money from their dice pips.
When a casino scores, every player with dice in that casino earns $1M per pip showing on their dice, regardless of whether they are boss. A die showing 6 earns $6M.
If any player reaches 90 or more victory points at any time, they win immediately. The game does not continue to the end of the current turn.

A contiguous group of same-color tiles on the board. All tiles must connect via shared edges (not corners). A casino cannot be voluntarily subdivided. It has one boss and can contain dice from multiple players.

The player with the highest single die value in a casino. The boss scores VP, and only the boss can sprawl or remodel that casino. If multiple players tie for highest die, the tied dice are rerolled immediately until one player wins.

When two adjacent casinos become the same color (via remodel or building), they merge into one casino. The boss of the merged casino is the player with the highest single die across all dice in the combined casino. Mergers cannot be undone.
Two lots are adjacent if they share a full edge (side), not just a corner. Adjacency determines sprawl targets, casino contiguity, merger triggers, and Strip adjacency for Pay The Strip scoring.

The main road running through the center of the board. Lots adjacent to The Strip are premium locations. When a Pay The Strip card is drawn, all casinos with at least one tile adjacent to The Strip score points and pay money, regardless of color.

Special property cards in the deck. When drawn, instead of one color scoring, ALL casinos adjacent to The Strip score and pay. Boss scores VP per tile, all players earn per pip. No specific lot is claimed from these cards.

When a player wins a gamble at your casino (and you are boss), you may lay off half the winnings. This means half the winner's payout is taken from the bank by the boss instead of going to the gambler. Reduces the gambler's effective winnings.

A single rectangular space on the board, identified by a block letter (A-F) and number. Each lot has a printed build cost and color. Lots can hold a marker (unbuilt, owned) or a casino tile with dice (built).
A group of lots on the board labeled A through F. Blocks organize the board geographically. In 2-player games, the entire F block is disabled and cards showing F lots are skipped.
The dollar amount printed on each lot. Paid to the bank when building on that lot. Sprawling costs double the build cost. Ranges from $1M to $9M depending on the lot.
Step 1 of each turn. The active player draws a property card, claims the lot, all players collect $1M per marker, and casinos of the drawn color (or Strip-adjacent casinos) score and pay.
Step 2 of each turn. The active player may perform actions in any order and any number of times: Build, Sprawl, Remodel, Reorganize, Trade (gamble limited to once). Ends when the player passes.
In a 2-player game, the entire F block is removed from play. When a card showing an F lot is drawn, discard it and draw again until a non-F card appears. All other rules remain the same.
